But we're trained to sort of think about this, is who says they're ranked? Who says they're number one or number two or number three? And even when it's an official ranking that nobody knows what the official ranking means, it gets used because it's official. So in the car business, what is it? I forget the name of the thing. The... Bald... well, that's industrial. JD Power, yeah. One of you, you both speak, you think one of you, you deal with the auto industry, you guys. The JD Power rankings for safest and most popular and best of this and best of that. And all the car companies use it in their commercials. And I guarantee you there's not a consumer on the planet that knows what the hell JD Power is. What is it? Is it a person? Is it an organization? Is it consumer reports? No, it's a company that sells awards to the auto industry. That's what it is. But it's official, right? And so who ranks you? The TV commercials for lawyers.com. What do they say? They say, Martindale Hubble rankings. You think the public has that button? It's official, so it's got to be important. So that's a real important word when you talk about status is who. Who is doing the ranking? And then last, renown, which is a little different than fame. It's not quite as demanding, but it's important is being known, right? It's no good if you're number one or number eight out of 100 and nobody knows it. And it's no good if you're ranked by somebody else and nobody knows it. You might as well not have the status to start with. So being known and then being known for what and being known for what to whom is where all the money is. So if you stop thinking about this, being known for whatever, but to the wrong people, like people who can't give you sufficient money to make seven figures, well, that's no good. That's not important, right? Being known by the right people, but being known for the wrong thing, that's not particularly productive, but not being known at all is maybe the worst of any of it. So we got to be concerned with that one as well. So if you look at who is big on being known, we would get to Trump, right? And so Trump has figured out, I mean, Trump is in the business of being Trump. And in the last handful of years, that really became his entire business, and he's pretty good at it. And he's like an ambulance chasing attorney, right? If there is something that he can get himself on TV to comment about, he'll be there. And he's in the obvious places and the unobvious places. People don't know he works at QVC, unless you watch QVC, but he's on QVC selling stuff, which he's also pretty good at. If there's no natural thing, he'll pick a fight with somebody. Rosie, and if he runs out of other people, Rosie's sort of now perennially available. Arguably he ought to be paying her. But it's important, I think, to know when you see somebody like this, that he hasn't invented anything. There's nothing new under the sun there, and as a matter of fact, there's a fairly reliable model. I'm going to use Ogilvy as an example quite a bit during the three days, because Ogilvy's like the ad guy of ad guys who I studied when I was beginning to be interested in advertising. And maybe above all other advertising people, Ogilvy advertised Ogilvy. And just as...
